task: I’m putting together a short checklist for inspecting a used piece of equipment before I decide whether to buy it. What are the first things you’d verify so the decision is based on what’s actually there and not just someone’s claim or a sloppy inspection?

turns.agent_response:
Start with **identity, condition, proof, and function**.

## First things to verify
1. **Exact identity**
   - model
   - serial number
   - asset tag, if any
   - match it to the listing

2. **Visible condition**
   - leaks
   - rust / corrosion
   - missing parts
   - damage
   - signs of overheating or repairs

3. **Proof of maintenance**
   - dated service records
   - hours at service
   - actual work listed
   - receipts or invoices tied to the unit

4. **Basic operation**
   - does it power on / start
   - does it run steadily
   - any alarms, fault codes, or odd noises

5. **Load / real-world test**
   - does it do the job under expected use
   - does it hold up when loaded

## What to document
- nameplate photo
- hour meter photo
- photos of defects
- receipts/invoices
- video of startup and run
- any fault codes
- readings if you can measure them

## Red flags
- no serial/model match
- “recently serviced” with no paperwork
- fresh paint hiding wear
- starts but won’t hold under load
- seller won’t allow testing
- maintenance records don’t match the wear

## Simple rule
If the paperwork, wear, and performance don’t line up, trust the mismatch more than the claim.

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page buy/no-buy checklist**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. But for me I’d tighten it a bit more around **what can be independently verified**.

First pass would be:
- **ID match**: nameplate, serial, model, hour meter, asset tag
- **Physical state**: corrosion, oil/coolant leaks, heat damage, missing fasteners, tamper marks, fresh paint hiding stuff
- **Maintenance proof**: actual dated records, not just “serviced recently” nonsense
- **Function**: start/run, alarms, fault codes, abnormal noise/vibration
- **Load behavior**: verify it under the condition it’ll actually see, not just idle on the seller’s floor

What I’d want added is:
- **consumables/wear items**: belts, bearings, batteries, filters, seals, contacts, anything with obvious life left
- **power/connection points**: terminals, plugs, breaker condition, wiring, grounding if relevant
- **history of failure**: what was replaced, why, and whether the same fault keeps coming back

And I’d keep one hard rule: if the seller won’t let you test it or the records don’t line up with the actual wear, treat that as a defect. Not a minor one.

If you want, I can make it into a tighter **buy / pass** checklist you can use on-site.

turns.agent_response:
Start with **identity, evidence, and function**.

## First things to verify
1. **Exact identity**
   - model
   - serial number
   - hour meter
   - asset tag, if present
   - match it to the listing

2. **Physical condition**
   - leaks
   - rust / corrosion
   - missing parts
   - damage
   - heat damage
   - tamper marks
   - fresh paint hiding wear

3. **Maintenance proof**
   - dated service records
   - hours at service
   - what was actually done
   - receipts or invoices tied to the unit

4. **Wear items and connections**
   - belts
   - bearings
   - batteries
   - filters
   - seals
   - contacts
   - terminals / plugs / wiring / grounding if relevant

5. **Basic operation**
   - start / run
   - alarms
   - fault codes
   - abnormal noise / vibration

6. **Load behavior**
   - test it under the condition it will actually see
   - not just idle on the seller’s floor

7. **Failure history**
   - what was replaced
   - why it was replaced
   - whether the same fault keeps coming back

## What to document
- nameplate photo
- hour meter photo
- photos of defects
- receipts / invoices
- video of startup and run
- fault codes
- readings if you can measure them

## Hard rule
If the seller won’t let you test it, or the records don’t line up with the wear, treat that as a defect.

## Red flags
- no serial/model match
- “recently serviced” with no dated proof
- fresh paint hiding wear
- starts but won’t hold under load
- records don’t match actual condition
- repeated fault history with no clear fix

If you want, I can turn this into a **tight buy / pass on-site checklist**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s basically it.

This is the version I’d actually use:
- **ID match**: nameplate, serial, model, hour meter, asset tag
- **Physical state**: leaks, corrosion, heat damage, missing fasteners, tamper marks, fresh paint, broken or patched parts
- **Maintenance proof**: dated records, invoices, actual work done, service hours
- **Wear items / consumables**: belts, bearings, batteries, filters, seals, contacts, terminals, plugs, wiring, grounding if relevant
- **Function**: start, run, alarms, fault codes, noise, vibration
- **Load behavior**: test it under real use, not just idle
- **Failure history**: what failed, what got replaced, and whether it came back

Hard rule still stands: if the seller blocks testing, or the paperwork doesn’t match the wear, that’s a defect. I’d count it against the unit immediately.

That’s the checklist. Clean enough.
